actions.thought: J3o is a very weak, unplayable hand. Unsuited, disconnected, and with a low kicker, it falls completely outside of any reasonable button raising range. The only correct move is to fold and look for a better spot to invest chips.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s 2c, which is an extremely weak hand. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind. While it only costs 50 more to call, 42o has very poor equity and playability post-flop, especially out of position. It is best to fold this hand and wait for a better spot rather than committing more chips with a marginal holding.

I am holding pocket Jacks (Js Jc) on the button, which is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. My goal is to raise for value and potentially take down the pot early or narrow the field. A standard 3x open raise to 300 is appropriate here.

My hand is 9-5 offsuit, which is a weak holding. I am in the Small Blind and out of position against the Button's raise. Calling would require committing more chips to play a weak hand in a disadvantaged position. Folding is the standard, disciplined play to preserve my stack.
Facing a 3x open from the Button with an offsuit 76, which plays poorly out of position. This is a clear fold.

The hand [2c, Th] is extremely weak and lacks significant playability or high-card value. As the button, while I have position, opening with such a bottom-range hand is statistically unfavorable against two opponents. It is early in the tournament, so preserving my stack and waiting for better cards is the most prudent strategy.

With 9hTs in the small blind after the button folds, I have a decent but marginal hand. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the big blind and gives me fold equity, allowing me to potentially win the 150 pot outright. If called, I have a connected hand that can flop well.

I hold pocket tens in the Big Blind. The Small Blind has opened with a raise to 300. Pocket tens are very strong in a blind-vs-blind situation and are ahead of a significant portion of the Small Blind's opening range. I will 3-bet to 900 total to build the pot for value, gain initiative, and discourage the opponent from realizing equity with overcards.

I opened T9o from the small blind as a steal attempt, but the big blind has 3-bet to 900. T9o is a marginal offsuit connector that plays very poorly out of position against a 3-betting range. Calling 600 more chips here is -EV because of our positional disadvantage and the relative weakness of our hand. The best play is to simply let this one go and fold.

Holding a suited King on the Button in a 3-handed game is a good opportunity to open for a raise. I will raise to 2.5 big blinds to try and steal the blinds, or at least play a pot in position with flush and top pair potential.

I am in the Small Blind holding 7d 9c, which is a weak offsuit hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Defending this hand out of position against a Button range is generally unprofitable, especially with the Big Blind still to act. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to conserve my stack.

I am in the big blind with a very weak hand, 7-2 offsuit. The opponent raised from the button to 2.5x the big blind. Calling 150 into a pot of 400 with such a poor range is mathematically unfavorable and puts me in a difficult spot post-flop. Folding is the disciplined choice here to preserve my stack.

Ten-Nine offsuit is a standard open-raising hand from the button in a 3-handed game. I want to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot while playing in position. A raise to 2.5x the big blind is a standard opening size.

I hold 4c 3h, which is an extremely weak hand with poor equity. I am out of position in the small blind against a button raise. Calling or raising here would be ill-advised given the lack of playability of this hand and the disadvantage of being out of position. I will fold and conserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

Facing a 2.5x raise from an active Button, AJo is a strong hand that benefits from a 3-bet. A 3-bet will allow me to take the initiative, deny equity, and potentially extract value from worse hands like ATo, KQ, or smaller pocket pairs. I'll size my 3-bet to around 3.4x to negate my positional disadvantage and put pressure on the Button.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind after my button open, T9o is too weak to continue. While I am in position, the hand lacks the high-card strength or suitedness required to profitably call a 3-bet or 4-bet bluff. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Ace-Queen suited is a premium starting hand that plays well in position. As the button, I should take the initiative to build the pot and potentially isolate one of the blinds. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate to apply pressure.

Holding 4d 2h in the Small Blind facing a 3bb raise from the Button. This is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em with virtually zero playability or equity. Folding is the only logical choice.

I have AdQd, a premium hand, in the Big Blind. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button, which is a standard opening range. I will 3-bet to 1050 for value and to take initiative in the hand, as my hand is well ahead of a typical Button opening range and plays well post-flop if called.

Facing a 3-bet from the big blind, folding is too tight given the strength and playability of Ace-Queen suited. I am in position and have deep stacks, making calling the most prudent decision to see a flop and realize my equity. 4-betting at this stage risks committing unnecessary chips if behind a premium range, so I will call the raise of 750 to bring my total commitment to 1050 and proceed post-flop.

As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage on this board, which contains a pair of Jacks. My range includes hands like AA, KK, QQ, and AJ, which are very strong here. Flashlight's range for calling a 3-bet on the button likely contains medium pairs and suited connectors. I will place a continuation bet of 750 (approximately 1/3 of the pot) to maintain initiative, extract value from draws or weaker hands, and potentially force folds from hands that have equity against my Ace-high.

The board is paired (J-J-7) with a potential flush draw present. I hold the nut flush draw with two overcards, giving me significant equity. My opponent's small lead bet of 750 into a 2150 pot offers good immediate pot odds. Raising at this point would turn a strong drawing hand into a bluff on a board that might have already hit my opponent, whereas calling allows me to realize my equity and see a turn card cheaply while maintaining a manageable pot size.

The turn card is the 7 of clubs, making the board JJ77. This is a significant card because it changes the value of my Ace-Queen. On a JJ77 board, my hand (Ace-high) now beats any pocket pair lower than Jacks (88, 99, 1010) because the best five-card hand for those pairs would be the two pair on the board with their pocket pair as a kicker, whereas I have the two pair on the board with an Ace kicker (JJ77A). However, I still lose to any Jack or any Seven, which are well within the opponent's range for calling a 3-bet and a flop continuation bet. Since betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me (Jacks, Sevens, or AA/KK) and force out hands that I now beat, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a showdown or induce a bluff from missed heart draws.

The board is now paired twice (J-J-7-7), which significantly decreases the likelihood that my Ace-high is ahead if my opponent has even a small pair or high cards from their 3-betting range. Since my opponent checked the turn after leading the flop, they are likely either trapping with a Jack or a full house, or they are giving up with overcards like KK/QQ. Betting here would essentially turn my hand into a bluff against a range that likely connects with the board. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity for free, see the river, and potentially hit my flush draw or catch a favorable river card, while keeping the pot manageable against a strong opponent.

The river Queen improves my hand significantly, giving me the best possible two-pair (Queens and Jacks with an Ace kicker). However, the board is double-paired (JJ77), which means any Jack or Seven in Flashlight's range makes a full house. Flashlight called my 3-bet pre-flop and my continuation bet on the flop, which strongly suggests they could hold a Jack (like AJ, KJ, or J10) or a mid-pocket pair. The check-check on the turn indicates they might be slow-playing a monster or holding a marginal hand. By checking the river, I can safely realize my showdown value against missed draws and pocket pairs like 88-TT that I now beat, while avoiding a larger loss against a full house. Betting would likely only result in being called or raised by hands that have me beaten.

The board is heavily paired (J-J-7-7-Q). My Ace-Queen gives me two pair (Jacks and Queens), but with two pairs on the board, any Jack or Seven in my opponent's range would have Trips or a Full House, beating me. Given that my opponent checked the turn and the river, they are likely either trapped with a strong hand (like a full house or trips) or have given up on the hand. Betting into this board on the river is high-risk, as it would likely only be called by a better hand, while checking back allows me to realize the showdown value of my two pair and avoid walking into a potential trap.
7h 5s offsuit is a very weak hand to play from the button, especially facing two opponents. The expected value of playing this hand is negative. Folding is the optimal play.

I hold Ah 8h in the Small Blind. Since the Button has folded, it is a blind-vs-blind situation. Ah 8h is a very strong hand in this spot, well ahead of the Big Blind's random range. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and build the pot with a hand that has both high card value and nut flush potential.

With Q-2 offsuit in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind, I am significantly behind the opponent's opening range. It is early in the tournament, and defending with such a marginal holding out of position is negative expected value. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Jack-Nine offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a standard hand to open for a steal attempt to put pressure on the blinds. A 2.5x raise is a standard sizing that allows me to take control of the pot or win it pre-flop without risking too much if one of the blinds decides to 3-bet.

A-7 offsuit is a weak, marginal hand that performs poorly out of position. Against a button raise, calling would force me to play a potentially dominated hand out of position, which is a losing strategy over the long run. Folding allows me to preserve my stack of 97BB for a better, more advantageous spot.
87o is too weak to call a raise out of position, even against a late-position open from the Button. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve chips.

Holding Ten of Spades and Seven of Diamonds is a weak starting hand with very poor playability. In a three-handed game, playing such a marginal hand from the button is generally not profitable. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have pocket nines in the Small Blind and the Button folded. This is a very strong hand for a blind vs blind situation. I want to raise to build the pot and take the initiative, targeting a standard 3x raise. If the Big Blind 3-bets, my hand is still strong enough to defend or 4-bet.

In a 3-handed game, ranges are significantly wider. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300, and I am in the Big Blind with J8o. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600, giving me 2:1 pot odds. Most importantly, since the Button folded, I will have direct position on the Small Blind for the remainder of the hand. J8o is strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a Small Blind open in this spot, especially given the positional advantage post-flop.

The flop comes Kh 8c Kc, pairing the top card. This texture heavily favors my pre-flop raising range, which includes all the strong Kx combinations. My pocket nines currently hold marginal showdown value as an underpair to the Kings but over the 8. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot (200 chips) is optimal here to deny equity to overcard floats, charge any club flush draws, and represent a strong hand, while minimizing risk if I face resistance.

On a board of Kh-8c-Kc, I have middle pair (Eights) with a Jack kicker. The Small Blind (Pro) led out with a small continuation bet of 200 into a 600 pot (1/3 pot). This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, but my 8 is far too strong to fold to a single small bet, especially with position. I am getting 4:1 odds to call. I will call to keep the pot controlled and see how the turn card changes the board state, as raising would likely only isolate me against Kings or better pocket pairs.

The turn 8h double-pairs the board to K-K-8-8, which severely downgrades the value of my pocket nines. Since my best 5-card hand is now K-K-8-8-9, I am losing to any opponent holding a Ten, Jack, Queen, or Ace, as their higher card will play as the kicker. I no longer beat A-high, Q-high, J-high, or T-high. Betting here would turn my hand into a pure bluff, and since the opponent called the flop, they likely have at least a piece of the board, a draw, or a high card that currently has me beat. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot, and I will likely fold to a bet.

On a board of Kh-8c-Kc-8h, I have improved to a full house (Eights full of Kings). The Small Blind (Pro) has checked to me. This board is extremely polarized; either the opponent has a King (which gives them a higher full house: Kings full of Eights) or they have very little. By checking back, I control the pot size in case I am behind a King and allow the opponent to bluff the river or potentially value-bet a pocket pair that they might have folded to a turn bet. There are very few cards on the river that can hurt my hand, so slow-playing here is a high-EV line to maximize value from their bluffs or weaker holdings.

On the river, the 5c completes the club flush draw. My pocket Nines give me two pair, Kings and Nines, which is ahead of missed A-high floats and smaller pocket pairs (like 66 or 77). However, I lose to any flush, higher pocket pairs (TT-QQ), and any King or Eight that may have slowplayed the turn. Betting here has very little value as worse hands will likely fold and only better hands will call or raise. Checking allows me to control the pot, realize my showdown value, and gives the opponent a chance to bluff missed draws or air.

On a double-paired board of Kh-8c-Kc-8h-5c, I have Eights full of Kings (8-8-8-K-K). While this is a very strong hand, it is vulnerable to any King in the Small Blind's range, which would make Kings full of Eights (K-K-K-8-8). The Small Blind (Pro) raised pre-flop and continuation bet the flop, then checked both the turn and the river. This line could indicate a King that is playing cautiously or a hand like a pocket pair (99-QQ) that has lost confidence. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by hands I beat (like JJ or Ace-high), but I will certainly be called or raised by a King. By checking back, I ensure I win the pot against the opponent's bluffs and marginal holdings without risking a loss to a superior full house.

Ace-Queen offsuit is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. As the first to act on the Button, opening with a standard raise to 250 builds the pot, takes the initiative, and asserts pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven of clubs, a decent suited hand. The button (Pro) raised to 250. While I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand, the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot that will be 600 if the Big Blind also calls) make this a reasonable hand to defend. T7s has good playability on many flop textures. I will call and see the flop rather than 3-betting or folding.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. Facing a raise of 2.5 BB from the button with a caller in the small blind, the pot odds dictate a call. Folding would be too conservative, and 3-betting with A7o out of position against two players is not a strong play. Calling allows me to see a flop with a decent hand and closing the action ensures I see the board.

I have a flush draw on a 9-high board. Being first to act, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and see how the action unfolds. Checking allows me to evaluate the strength of my opponents' hands based on their betting patterns and keeps the pot manageable while I look for my flush or improved outs.

I am in the Big Blind on a flop of 6c-3d-9c with A-high. I have no pair and no significant draws. Betting here would be a bluff into two opponents, including the pre-flop aggressor. Since I have no equity to justify a bet and checking allows me to potentially see a free card or re-evaluate on the turn against minimal aggression, checking is the standard and correct play.

With Ace-Queen high and only a backdoor flush draw (having the Qc), on a 6c 3d 9c board, my hand doesn't have quite enough equity to justify a continuation bet into two opponents. Checking behind controls the pot size, prevents me from getting check-raised by flush draws or made hands, and allows me to see a free turn card where I could hit an Ace, a Queen, or pick up more equity.

The turn card is a Nine of spades, pairing the board. I still have a flush draw with the Ten-Seven of clubs. On the flop, everyone checked, suggesting that no one is likely holding a Nine or a strong overpair. By checking again, I can potentially see a free river card to complete my flush or evaluate the strength of my opponents if they decide to bet. Being out of position, checking is the safest way to control the pot size while drawing.

The board is 6c-3d-9c-9s and I hold Ace-high. Everyone checked the flop, indicating a lack of strong hands. With a paired board, betting into two opponents with only Ace-high is a low-percentage bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any reasonable hand. Checking is the safest play, allowing me to potentially realize my equity or get to a cheap showdown, while keeping the pot small as I have no significant hand to value bet.

Both opponents have checked twice, indicating weakness, but my Ace-Queen high still holds reasonable showdown value. Taking a free card to the river keeps the pot small and allows me to see if I hit an Ace or Queen. Betting here into two opponents might only get called by better hands, such as small pocket pairs or a slow-played 9. Checking is the safest and most standard option for pot control.

I have missed my flush draw and currently hold 10-high, which has almost zero showdown value. The action has been checked through the flop and turn by all three players, suggesting a very weak range for everyone. While a bluff could potentially take down the pot, I am out of position against two opponents. The river Jack might have improved someone's hand (like QJ, KJ, AJ or JT), and since the pot is relatively small, I prefer to check and see if I can get to a cheap showdown or simply fold if someone bets, rather than risking chips on a multi-way bluff this early in the tournament.

With everyone checking through the flop and turn, it is highly likely that no one has connected strongly with this board. My Ace-high is quite strong in this specific scenario, as opponents likely would have bet a pair or better. Checking behind is the optimal play to reach a cheap showdown, as I have no value to bet and no reason to turn my hand into a bluff.

Both opponents have checked the flop, turn, and river, suggesting they likely have marginal made hands or missed draws like a club flush draw or straight draw. My Ace-Queen high has solid showdown value in this spot. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands, such as a rivered Jack, or small pairs that decided to check down. Since worse hands will fold and better hands will call, checking behind to realize my showdown value is the most profitable decision.

I am on the button with 7s5s, a suited one-gapper. In a 3-handed game, this is a strong hand to open with, especially when folded to. Opening on the button allows me to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand with position. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to test the blinds.

I am in the small blind with Queen of Clubs and Trey of Hearts. This is a weak, unsuited hand and I am out of position. Calling a button raise from the small blind with this holding is mathematically negative expectation, and there is no reason to inflate the pot with a bluff given the lack of connectivity and the low equity of these cards. I will fold and conserve my stack.

97 offsuit is a weak, uncoordinated hand with very low equity against a standard button open. Facing a 3x raise from Flash, defending from the Big Blind here is highly unprofitable. Folding is the correct move to preserve our stack for better opportunities.

My starting hand, 2s8h, is extremely weak and provides no significant equity in a 3-handed game. As the button, I could technically open, but with such a poor hand, it is mathematically better to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable spot. There is no strategic reason to invest chips with a hand this low-ranking.

T9o is a marginal, unsuited connector. While stealing from the Small Blind is sometimes a viable option, playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind without a suit is not profitable enough, especially against a player who has shown a willingness to be aggressive. The best play is to fold and save the chips for better spots.
9d 5h is a very weak, unsuited hand. Even on the button, it does not have enough equity or playability to justify opening the pot. It is a clear fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Deuce of Spades (J2s). The action has folded to me. While J2s is a suited hand, it is quite weak for playing out of position against the Big Blind (Flashlight), especially since Flashlight has shown a tendency to defend their blind in previous hands. Given my high activity level in earlier hands, I expect Flashlight to defend even more aggressively now. Folding here is the most disciplined play to avoid a difficult post-flop situation with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with Ten-Seven offsuit. While 3-handed play allows for a wider opening range, T7o is generally too weak to open profitably, especially with active opponents in the blinds who have shown a willingness to 3-bet and defend. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With Flash (Button) having folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind with 9s8s in the Small Blind. This is a strong speculative hand, and completing the blind is a standard play to see a flop cheaply against the Big Blind.

Flashlight limped from the Small Blind. I have 82o in the Big Blind, which is a very weak hand. Since I already have my big blind in the pot and it costs nothing to close the pre-flop action, checking is the obvious and only standard play. I will see a free flop and fold to any aggression if I don't hit a miracle flop.

The flop is extremely dry and Ace-heavy, which significantly favors the big blind's range over mine. My 9-high is essentially air and lacks any meaningful equity, so betting would be a poor strategic choice. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small and avoid inflating it with a weak hand.

The flop brings two Aces and a club draw. We have absolute air with 8-high and no draw. Flashlight checked to us, and we have no reason to bluff on this texture. We will just check and take the free card.

The turn card (4d) does not improve my hand, and I am still holding 9-high. Checking is the standard play here as I am out of position and have very little equity. Betting into the opponent would only fold out hands that are already worse than mine (if any exist) and would likely be called by any Ace or pair, which have me crushed. I plan to check to keep the pot manageable and see the river cheaply.

Flashlight has checked the turn again. With 8-high on a double-Ace board and no draws, we have absolutely zero equity. Although we could try a delayed bluff here, the pot is tiny and there's no reason to risk chips when we can just take another free card. Checking behind is the safest and most standard play.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s8s. The board is Ad Ac 7c 4d 5c. I have 9-high with no showdown value. Both I and the Big Blind (Pro) have checked through the flop and turn, suggesting neither of us has a strong hand (or perhaps Pro is trapping with an Ace). Given the board state and the lack of aggression from my opponent, my hand has zero equity at showdown. Attempting a bluff is highly risky against an unknown range that checked down, and likely to be called by any pair (which are prevalent in a Big Blind's calling range). Checking is the safest and most logical play to minimize losses.

Flashlight checked the river on a board with two Aces and three clubs. We have 8-high and absolutely no showdown value. However, the pot is only 200 chips from a pre-flop limp. Attempting a bluff here would require risking an unnecessary amount of chips to win a tiny pot, and Flashlight could easily call with any weak pair, King-high, or a slow-played hand. Giving up and taking the free showdown is the most prudent play to preserve our stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine offsuit, which is a strong hand for this position in a three-handed game. Raising here allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, or play with position if called. A standard open raise to 250 is appropriate to establish control of the hand.

I am dealt 73o in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise to 2.5x from the Button, there is absolutely no reason to call or raise. This is a clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jc 8h. Flashlight raised from the Button to 250. Pro folded in the Small Blind. I need to call 150 more into a pot that will be 550. This gives me pot odds of approximately 2.67:1, requiring about 27.3% equity to break even. Against a typical 3-handed Button opening range, J8o has significantly more equity than that (usually around 40-45%). Although I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, the price is too attractive to fold. Calling is the standard play here to see a flop.

I have bottom pair (8s) on a board that is quite favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range, which contains many Kx and Tx hands. Being out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading here doesn't accomplish much as better hands will call and I might get raised off my equity. I'll check and evaluate based on Flashlight's action and bet sizing.

The board of 8c-Kc-Ts is very coordinated and favors a big blind defending range, which likely includes many broadway cards, suited connectors, and pairs that hit this flop well. My hand, Ace-Nine offsuit, has no pair and no significant draw, making it weak on this texture. Betting here would essentially be turning my hand into a bluff, which is likely to be called by better hands or raised. Checking behind is the most standard and profitable play, allowing me to control the pot size and take a free card to see if I can improve.

On the turn, the board is 8c Kc Ts 4d and I have bottom pair with an 8. The pre-flop aggressor, Flashlight, checked back on the flop, which often signals a marginal hand or a complete miss. While I might have the best hand, betting into a board with a King and a Ten out of position is risky, as I could be value-owning myself against a slow-played King or a Ten. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see if Flashlight intends to bluff or check along again. If Flashlight bets, I will evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the most cautious and flexible play.

The board texture is 8c-Kc-Ts-4d and my opponent has checked to me again on the turn. I hold Ace-Nine offsuit, which lacks a pair, a straight draw, or a flush draw. Betting here would essentially be turning my hand into a bluff against a range that likely includes Kings, Tens, or Eights which will not fold. Checking behind is the most standard and profitable play to control the pot size and get to the river to see if my Ace-high has showdown value or if I can improve, without bloating the pot with a weak holding.

On the river, the board is 8c-Kc-Ts-4d-Ac. I have a pair of eights. The action has been checked through since the flop, which suggests both Flashlight and I have marginal hands. The Ace on the river is a double-edged sword; it could have improved Flashlight's likely 'check-down' range (like Ax hands), or it could be a complete blank. Betting now doesn't serve much purpose as I am unlikely to get called by worse or fold out a better hand (like a King or a newly made pair of Aces). Checking is the best option to reach showdown cheaply and potentially win against missed draws or lower pocket pairs.

I have improved to a pair of Aces on the river, but the board is heavily coordinated with three clubs (8c, Kc, Ac), making a flush possible, along with straight possibilities (QJ). Since the opponent checked to me, betting for value is risky as they might be check-trapping with a flush or a set, or they might fold all worse hands. Checking behind allows me to realize my showdown value safely and avoids being check-raised off the best hand.
Q5o is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Even from the Button, it is not strong enough to open-raise or call with. The best decision is to simply fold and save chips for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. After the button (Pro) folded, the action is on me. As8h is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario. Standard strategy dictates raising here to either win the blinds immediately or to play a pot with range advantage and initiative. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard sizing for this position to put pressure on the Big Blind.

T-4 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position against a raise. Defending the big blind here would be negative expected value. Folding is the correct disciplined play to maintain stack health for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 5d 2d. While it is a suited hand and I am in the best position, 5-2 is extremely weak even in a 3-handed game. I have been active in opening from the button in previous hands, so my opponents might be more inclined to defend or 3-bet. Folding here is the most disciplined play to maintain a solid image and avoid getting into difficult post-flop situations with a hand that has very little equity.

The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. While Jack-Five offsuit is a marginal hand, the pot odds of calling 50 to win the 100 already in the pot justify completing the small blind to see a flop, rather than folding and conceding the pot immediately.

With Jack-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind against a Small Blind limp, checking is a solid and standard play. We get to see a free flop in position with a hand that plays reasonably well post-flop, avoiding building a bigger pot pre-flop with an offsuit broadway hand.

The flop is 9d-7s-Ks, which misses my Jh-5s entirely. As the small blind acting first, I have no pair and no real draw, making this a classic 'check' situation. I will evaluate the opponent's action to decide if a continuation or fold is appropriate on later streets.

With a gutshot straight draw to the 8 and potential overcards to the middle pairs, checking behind on the flop is a safe line after the Small Blind checks. It allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially improve on the turn without investing chips on a weak draw.

I hold a pair of Jacks (Jh), which is a decent hand, but the board is getting wet with multiple straight and flush draws (spades, connectors). Since my opponent (Pro) checked the flop and has not initiated any action on the turn, I should maintain a controlled approach. Betting risks isolating myself against hands that have me beat (such as a King) or folding out hands I might beat, while checking allows me to realize showdown value or evaluate the river action cheaply.

The turn gives me middle pair (Jacks) and an open-ended straight draw (any 8 or Q makes a straight), but the board now has three spades. Since I don't hold a spade, my hand is extremely vulnerable to flushes, and I could already be behind to a King or a better Jack. Betting half-pot serves as an excellent semi-bluff, applying pressure to fold out their better high-card hands while building the pot in case I complete my flush on the river.

The opponent (Pro) checked the flop, indicating they likely didn't have a strong King or were playing cautiously. By betting on the paired turn (4-8-K-4), they are representing a Four, an Eight, or potentially a King. My hand, Queen-Jack high, has essentially zero showdown value and no equity against any pair. Investing more chips into a pot with a hand that loses to nearly the entire range of my opponent—and on a board texture that favors a calling range containing Kings and eights—is mathematically incorrect. I have no pair and no draw, making this an easy fold.

I am in the button position with Jack-Nine offsuit, which is a marginal hand that does not warrant opening or calling in this spot. The field is competitive, and I prefer to wait for a stronger starting hand to enter a pot, especially considering the current stacks.

J4o is a very weak offsuit hand. From the Small Blind, it is generally best to fold this rather than complete or raise, as I would be playing out of position against the Big Blind with a marginal holding.
I am on the button with 7h 8s (87 offsuit). This is a weak unsuited connector, and in a 3-handed game, it's generally best to fold this rather than trying to steal or limp. I will fold.

Holding King-Four offsuit in the Small Blind after the Button has folded is a situation where I should typically open the pot. King-high has significant equity against a random Big Blind range in a three-handed game. By raising to 300, I put pressure on Flashlight to fold their weaker hands while maintaining the initiative if they decide to call. Given the current stack depths, a standard 3x raise is appropriate.

I am holding 2c 7d, which is a bottom-tier hand. Calling a raise out of position with such a weak holding is negative expected value. Given the stack depths and my position, it is prudent to fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with Jack-Eight suited (J8d). This is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game, offering good post-flop playability and straight/flush potential. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or see a flop with positional advantage.

I am in the small blind with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. The button has opened for a raise, and playing this hand out of position against an aggressor is a losing strategy in the long run. Folding is the only prudent decision here to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3bb raise from the Button, J8 suited is a marginal hand. Playing it out of position against a strong pre-flop range will often lead to difficult situations on later streets. While it has some playability, folding is the most solid and lowest-variance choice to preserve the stack.

I am in the button position with Ace-Nine offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Opening with a raise is the optimal strategy to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or force my opponents into a difficult spot.

I am in the Small Blind with K5o, which is a very weak offsuit hand. Flashlight has opened to 3x the big blind from the Button. Calling out of position with this hand will lead to difficult post-flop spots, and raising as a bluff is unnecessary when I can simply fold and wait for better opportunities.

KQo is a premium hand in 3-handed play and is significantly ahead of a standard button opening range. By 3-betting to 1000, I am playing for value and taking the initiative in the hand, which is often preferable to calling out of position. This size puts pressure on the button and may result in winning the pot pre-flop or simplifying post-flop play.

Flash 3-bet from the big blind after my initial raise on the button. Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong hand in 3-handed play, and given I have already committed 300, calling the additional 700 to see a flop is mathematically sound. I want to maintain my position advantage and see if I can hit an Ace or a nine, or evaluate the board texture if I miss.

I have flopped top pair with the second-best kicker (KQ) and I hold the Queen of Clubs, giving me the second-nut flush draw on a monotone board. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage. I will lead with a continuation bet to get value from weaker Kings, smaller pairs with a club, or lone high clubs, while also protecting my hand against potential draws.

The flop is 3c-Kc-4c, which misses my Ace-Nine offsuit entirely. I have no pair, no flush draw, and no meaningful straight draw. My opponent showed significant strength by 3-betting pre-flop and leading out on the flop. While I have position, calling a bet here with only Ace-high is essentially a blind float that lacks sufficient equity to be profitable. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

King-Queen suited is a premium starting hand. Being on the Button, I want to open with a standard raise to build the pot, assert positional advantage, and either take the blinds down immediately or isolate against one opponent in position.

I am holding 9-6 offsuit in the small blind. This is a very weak hand to defend against a button raise from 'Pro'. Playing out of position with a marginal hand like this is generally unprofitable, so I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Pro (Button) has opened for 250. I am in the Big Blind with Kc 5c. The pot odds are excellent (150 to call into a total pot of 400). Given the suited nature of my hand and the favorable pot odds, calling is the standard and correct strategic play to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind and have flopped top pair (Kings) with a flush draw. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play. This allows the aggressor to potentially c-bet, which I can then respond to, or check behind, allowing me to take control or see a turn card cheaply.

I have top pair with a strong kicker on a draw-heavy board (two clubs and a Jack). Since Flashlight checked to me, it's a great spot to make a continuation bet to extract value from flush draws, straight draws, weaker Kings, or Jacks, while protecting my equity. A bet of around half the pot accomplishes this effectively.

I have flopped top pair (Kings) with a flush draw on a board of 4c Jc Ks. This is a very strong hand, and with the opponent betting 250 into a pot of 500, I have a clear opportunity to raise for value and protection. Raising to 750 puts pressure on any weaker holdings, draws, or middle pairs the opponent might have, and allows me to build a significant pot while I have equity.

Flashlight's check-raise on this draw-heavy flop (4c Jc Ks) could indicate a strong value hand like a set or two pair, but their range also includes numerous semi-bluffs such as club flush draws and straight draws. With King-Queen, I have top pair and a strong kicker, making a fold far too tight. However, re-raising would overplay my hand and isolate me against the value portion of their range. Calling is the most balanced play, keeping their bluffs in the pot and allowing me to leverage my position to evaluate the turn card.

The Ace of Diamonds on the turn is a significant scare card that potentially improves the opponent's range, especially if they have an Ace. With top pair (Kings) and a flush draw, checking is a prudent strategic choice. It allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the river while preserving my equity. If the opponent bets, I can decide whether to proceed based on the size of their wager and my pot odds.

The Ace on the turn is a scare card that completes some draws and hits hands that might have floated or check-raised the flop as semi-bluffs, such as Ace-high flush draws. Since Flashlight check-raised the flop and now checks the turn, betting here would turn my second pair into a bluff, and I would likely only be called by better hands like an Ace, two pair, or a set. Checking behind controls the pot size, allows me to realize my equity (including a gutshot to a Broadway straight with a Ten), and keeps Flashlight's missed draws in their range for a potential river decision.

I have successfully made a King-high flush on the river with my Kc and 5c on a board of 4c Jc Ks Ad 9c. Having checked the turn, I am now in a strong position to extract value from my opponent's range, which could include two-pair combinations or straight holdings that may feel compelled to call a value bet. A bet of 1000 represents approximately half of the 2050 pot, a standard sizing for extracting value without overplaying the hand.

The river brings the 9 of clubs, completing the obvious flush draw from the flop, and the Ace on the turn already downgraded my Kings to second pair. Furthermore, straight draws like QT have also hit. Flashlight's flop check-raise followed by a turn check and river bet strongly polarizes their range to hands that either improved to a flush or straight, or an Ace trying to extract value. With just a pair of Kings and no club, my hand is a pure bluff-catcher that beats almost nothing in their value range. Since very few missed draws are left that would take this line, folding is the only logical choice.

I am on the Button with 7c 6c, a strong suited connector. In 3-handed play, this is a standard opening hand to exert pressure on the blinds and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability. I will raise to 300, which is consistent with my previous opening sizes.

Flash is on the button and has been aggressive with their opening range. I am in the small blind with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a solid hand to defend against a steal attempt. While playing out of position post-flop is suboptimal, the pot odds are attractive and folding would be too tight given the stack sizes and our history.

With J9s in the Big Blind getting 3.5:1 pot odds after a button raise and small blind call, we have a great price to close the action. J9s plays well multi-way and can flop strong draws, straights, or flushes.

The flop is very dry with a paired board (Ks-Kc). I have Queen-high with no draw, which is extremely weak here. With three players in the hand and the board being highly unlikely to hit my range, betting would be an ineffective bluff. Checking is the only logical play to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on the action from the other two players.

With Jack-high and a backdoor club draw on a paired King board, I have very little equity and no immediate draw. The best play is to check to the preflop raiser and see what they do. Betting into two players here with air is not profitable.

The flop is Ks-3d-Kc, a very dry, paired board. Although I was the pre-flop aggressor, betting into two opponents on this texture is risky with only 7-high and no real draw. By checking, I control the size of the pot and can see a turn card for free. If both opponents check again on the turn, I may consider a delayed continuation bet to take the pot then.

The board texture remains extremely dry with Kings paired on the flop. My Queen-Jack high hand has no showdown value against any made hand, including a simple pair of twos or threes. Since the action checked through on the flop, there is a possibility that nobody has connected strongly with the Kings. However, betting as a bluff into two opponents here is high-risk and unlikely to succeed, as a pair of Kings or even a pocket pair would be difficult to force a fold from. Checking allows me to keep the pot small and maintain flexibility to fold if either opponent bets on the turn or river.

With Jack high on a paired King board and no draws remaining, I have very little equity. Since it is checked to me, checking is the clear and standard play. There is no reason to bluff into two players with this weak holding.

The board is extremely dry (Ks-3d-Kc-2h) and paired. I was the pre-flop aggressor and checked back on the flop. Both opponents have checked to me again on the turn. Their repeated checking suggests a lack of a King or any significant hand. Since I have 7-high and no showdown value, this is a prime opportunity for a delayed continuation bet to take down the pot. A bet of 450 (half pot) should be sufficient to fold out hands like Ace-high or small pocket pairs that are unsure of where they stand.

With no pair, no draw, and holding only Queen-high on a board of Ks-3d-Kc-2h, my hand has zero showdown value. Flash has opened the betting on the turn after checking the flop, which could indicate a continuation bet or a slow-play on the flop that they are now trying to extract value from. With Pro still to act behind me and a very dry board, calling is mathematically incorrect as I have no equity to improve and cannot call down any subsequent bets on the river. Folding is the only sensible move to preserve my stack.

With Jack-high and no draws on a paired board, I have essentially no equity against a bet. Flash's delayed turn bet puts pressure on my weak hand. Given that I have no realistic chance of improving on the river to win the pot, folding is the only profitable play.

I am in the button position with Ace-Three offsuit. This is a weak starting hand, especially in a 3-handed game against relatively active opponents. While I am on the button, the risk of getting into a difficult post-flop situation with a dominated ace is too high. Given the stack depths and the aggressive play styles of the other participants, folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to preserve my stack for a better spot.
With Q6o in the Small Blind and the Button folding, my hand is too weak to play optimally against the Big Blind. Folding is the standard and safest decision to preserve my stack.

Ah Qs is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Being on the button, I want to raise to build the pot, extract value, and take the betting initiative. A standard open-raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) accomplishes these goals while providing poor odds for the blinds to call with marginal holdings.

In a 3-handed game, hand ranges are significantly wider than in full-ring play. Pro is opening from the button, which likely represents a very wide range of hands. Although Ace-Trey offsuit (A3o) is a marginal hand to play out of position, the price to call is 200 chips into a 400-chip pot (giving me 2:1 odds). Given the stack depths and the aggressive nature of the button's opening range, A3o has enough equity to see a flop. I will call and evaluate based on the flop texture, while remaining cautious about my weak kicker if an Ace appears.

Ace-Jack suited is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. With a raise from the button and a call from the small blind, I am getting favorable pot odds to see a flop. While 3-betting to squeeze is a consideration to isolate the button, calling allows me to control the pot size and play post-flop in a multi-way pot with a hand that has good high-card strength and nut flush potential. Keeping the pot manageable while out of position is the prudent approach here.

On this low, dry flop of 7s-2s-4d, my Ace-high with a backdoor straight draw has some potential but is currently very weak. Since I am first to act in a three-way pot, leading out into the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) is generally not advisable without a strong hand or a significant draw. Checking allows me to see how the other players react. If Pro bets, I will have to evaluate based on his sizing, but for now, checking is the most standard and disciplined play to keep the pot small with a marginal holding.

I hold the nut flush draw with two overcards on a 7s-2s-4d board. Both opponents have checked to me, which signals potential weakness. Betting here is a standard semi-bluff; it puts pressure on their marginal holdings, gives me a chance to win the pot immediately, and builds a larger pot for when I hit my strong outs.

I missed the flop completely with just Ace-Queen high. The big blind has donk-bet into me and the small blind, which indicates strength or a strong draw on this 7s 2s 4d board. Without a pair or a strong draw, and with another player still to act behind me, folding is the correct and safest play to preserve my stack.

I have Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw to the wheel (A-2-3-4-5) on a 7s-2s-4d board. After I checked, Flashlight (Big Blind) bet 400 into a pot of 1150, and the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has already folded. I am receiving pot odds of nearly 4:1 (400 to win 1550). Given these favorable odds, my four outs to a straight, and the possibility that an Ace could be the best hand if it hits, a call is justified to see the turn. I am closing the action on this street, which simplifies the decision.

On the turn, the King of Hearts is an overcard that doesn't significantly change the board texture for my specific hand, Ace-Trey. I still have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Five) and Ace-high. Since Flashlight led into the field on the flop and I am out of position, checking is the most prudent move. It allows me to see how Flashlight responds to the King. If he checks back, I might have some showdown value or a chance to realize my equity. If he bets, I will have to evaluate the price against my draw and the likelihood that my Ace-high is currently the best hand.

I hold the nut flush draw on a board of 7s-2s-4d-Kh. Flash has checked to me again on the turn. Since he called my flop bet, he likely has some piece of the board or a draw, and betting again into him when I am only on a draw might be inefficient. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity and see the river card for free, preserving my stack while still having the potential to hit the nut flush on the river.

On the river, I have improved to a pair of Treys with an Ace kicker. The board texture is quite dangerous as the 3s completes a potential spade flush. Flashlight led the flop and then checked the turn, which often indicates a drawing hand or a marginal made hand like a 7 or 4. By checking, I am looking to realize my showdown value. Betting would not accomplish much, as better hands (like a 7, a King, or a flush) will call, and worse hands will fold. If Flashlight checks behind, I may win against a missed straight draw like 5-6. If he bets, I will have to evaluate the size and the likelihood of a bluff versus the completed flush.

I have rivered the nut flush with the Ace of spades on a board of 7s-2s-4d-Kh-3s. Flash has checked to me, and there is no reason to check behind when holding the absolute nuts. The goal is to maximize value from whatever holding Flash might have, such as a king, two pair, or a smaller flush. A value bet of roughly half the pot should be sufficient to induce a call.

On the river, the board texture has become quite coordinated with three spades and a possible straight. I have a pair of Treys with an Ace kicker. Flashlight led the flop but checked the turn, which often indicates a capped range or a draw that didn't improve on the turn. The 3s on the river completes the spade flush but also gives me a pair. Flashlight's bet of 800 into a pot of 1550 gives me roughly 3:1 pot odds (calling 800 to win 2350). Given the favorable odds and the possibility that Flashlight is bluffing with a missed straight draw (like 5-6) or a lower pair that he's turning into a bluff to represent the flush, I will call to realize my showdown value.

I am on the button with pocket threes in a three-handed game. This is a strong enough hand to open for a raise to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds. Small pairs are valuable for set-mining if called, and in short-handed play, they often hold up as the best hand or can be used to represent strength on many boards. I'll raise to a standard 3x blind amount.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Five offsuit. This is a weak hand, especially when playing out of position against an aggressive opener on the button. Calling 250 into a pot of 450 with such a marginal hand is negative expected value. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.
T2o is a bottom-tier starting hand with very little playability or equity. Facing a 3x button raise from Flash, folding is the only standard and profitable move.

Pocket nines are a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Being on the button, I want to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds or play a pot in position. A raise to 300 is a standard opening size.
83o is a very weak starting hand, well outside any defendable range. Facing a 3x raise from the button, the standard and only correct play is to fold.

I am in the big blind with 7s 4c. Flashlight raised to 300 from the button, and the small blind folded. 7-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand and does not justify a call or a raise even with the pot odds provided by the big blind defense. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with Jack-Five offsuit (Jh 5c). This is a very weak and disconnected hand, falling well outside of standard opening ranges from any position. The standard and optimal play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with T3s. Pro has folded from the button. The pot is currently 150 (100 from the Big Blind, 50 from my Small Blind) and it costs me 50 to call the Big Blind's 100. T3s is a weak hand, but getting 3:1 odds to see a flop against Flashlight (who has a large stack) is tempting. However, T3s doesn't play very well post-flop and Flashlight has been relatively active. Given the 150-hand limit and the need to protect my stack, I will fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot rather than completing and playing out of position.

I am on the button with 7h4h. While this is a suited hand, it is quite weak (7-high) and doesn't play particularly well against two opponents who have been reasonably active. My stack is around 90 big blinds, and I want to wait for better spots to commit chips, especially since both Flashlight and Pro are capable of putting on pressure. Folding is the most disciplined play here to conserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). I have Q4o, which is not a premium hand, but completing the small blind to see a flop heads-up is a standard, low-risk move given the 3-to-1 pot odds. I will call the additional 50 chips to bring my total investment for this street to 100.
With 64o in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind limp, my hand is too weak to raise for value or as a bluff. Checking my option to see a free flop is the standard and most profitable play.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a board with flush draw potential. Given that I checked pre-flop as the small blind and my opponent (Pro) checked behind, it is unlikely either of us has a premium hand. Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on my opponent's action, avoiding bloating a pot with a vulnerable kicker.

With an open-ended straight draw (4-5-6-7) on a Qd 7s 5s board and facing a check from the Small Blind in a limped pot, betting as a semi-bluff is a strong play. It allows me to fold out better high-card hands, take down the pot immediately, or build the pot in case I hit my straight on the turn.

I have top pair with a Queen, though my kicker (4) is weak. Given that the board has a flush draw and the Big Blind is betting the minimum or close to it, they could be betting with air, a draw, or a weaker hand. Folding here is too conservative, and raising would inflate the pot unnecessarily with a vulnerable hand. Calling allows me to keep the pot size manageable and see the turn card to reassess the situation.

I am in the Small Blind and act first on the turn. I have a pair of Queens with a weak kicker and a flush draw. The board is wet with three spades, making it dangerous. Checking allows me to control the pot size and act based on my opponent's decision, as betting here might commit me to a pot I cannot win if the opponent has a better hand or a made flush.

The turn gives us a 4-8 straight, but also puts a third spade on the board. Since we don't hold any spades, our straight is vulnerable to flushes, and there are higher straight possibilities if our opponent holds a 9. Betting here might only get called by better hands (like flushes or higher straights) or strong draws with high equity against us. Checking behind for pot control is the safest line, allowing us to evaluate the river and avoid getting check-raised out of the pot.

The river card is the 5d, pairing the board and giving me two pair, Queens and Fives. The board also features three spades, meaning a flush is possible. My opponent, Pro, checked the turn, suggesting they do not have a strong flush, but checking is still the most prudent action. A bet would only be called by better hands (like a better Queen, a flush, or a full house) or perhaps an occasional 5, and I would be folding out the hands I currently beat (missed straight draws or weaker pairs). Therefore, checking is the optimal choice to reach a cheap showdown.

On the river, we have an 8-high straight (4-5-6-7-8) on a paired board with three spades. This board is extremely well-connected, meaning our straight is very vulnerable. Any spade, any 9, or any full house/trips beats us. Because our opponent could easily be trapping with a better hand or calling only with hands that beat us, value betting here is extremely thin and risky. Checking behind allows us to safely realize our showdown value without exposing ourselves to a check-raise.

I am on the button with King-Queen suited, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. I should play aggressively to take control of the pot pre-flop and leverage my positional advantage. A standard open raise to 300 is the correct play here.

Jack-Nine offsuit is a weak hand to play from the Small Blind against a Button raise. The pot odds are not good enough to justify calling out of position, and the Big Blind is still to act behind me. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

I am in the big blind holding 93 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. The player on the button (Flashlight) has opened with a 3x raise. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 into a pot of 450), 93o has very poor playability and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. In a 3-handed game, ranges are wider, but this hand is still well below the threshold for a profitable defense. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
Holding 2d 6c on the button is one of the weakest possible hands in Texas Hold'em. This hand has virtually no playability or equity, making folding the only correct choice.

I am holding King-Jack offsuit in the Small Blind. Since the Button has folded, the action is on me. KJo is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game and is well ahead of an average random hand in the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot.

Flash raised to 300 from the small blind. Holding Ace-Ten offsuit in the big blind, this is a strong enough hand to defend against a steal attempt heads-up. Since the button folded, I am in a heads-up pot against the small blind. Calling is a solid, disciplined play to realize my equity in position and see how the board develops.

I started the hand by raising pre-flop with King-Jack offsuit from the Small Blind. The flop comes Queen of Clubs, Trey of Diamonds, Five of Clubs, which is a relatively dry board. Although I have missed this flop, I still hold two overcards and have the initiative as the pre-flop aggressor. This is a good spot for a continuation bet to represent a Queen or a high pocket pair and pressure the Big Blind, who likely has a wide defending range. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to take down the hand immediately or see how my opponent reacts.

I hold the Ace of Clubs on a two-club board (Qc, 3d, 5c), which gives me the nut flush draw and a strong overcard. The opponent has bet 300 into a 600 pot, giving me 3:1 pot odds. My equity with 9 flush outs plus the potential to hit an Ace is excellent here. Calling is the most disciplined and effective way to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable while I wait for further developments on the turn.

On this turn, the 4d doesn't significantly change the board texture, though it does add some straight possibilities (like 62 or A2). After my pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet were both called by Flashlight, it's likely they have a piece of the board—perhaps a Queen, a small pair, or a flush draw. King-high still has some showdown value in a three-handed game, and continuing to bet (barreling) into a range that called the flop is risky without improvement. I will check to control the pot size and hopefully see a free river card that could improve my hand to the top pair (King or Jack) or allow for a cheap showdown if Flashlight also checks back.

The opponent checked the turn, showing potential weakness after betting the flop. My hand, Ace-Ten, provides an overcard and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6 to complete a straight). By betting into the pot of 1200, I can take initiative, put pressure on the opponent's range, and potentially win the pot now or set up a strong river bluff if I improve my hand or if the opponent remains passive.

I am holding King-high on a board where my opponent has called both my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet. When I checked the turn to control the pot, Flashlight took the lead with a half-pot bet. My King-high has very little showdown value against a range that includes Queens, middle pairs, or completed draws. While I have some outs with a King or a Jack, I'm out of position and likely behind. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 5c 3d, which is a very weak starting hand. While the button is a good position to steal blinds, 53o is too far down the range to justify an open-raise, especially since both opponents (Flashlight and Pro) have shown willingness to defend and 3-bet in previous hands. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

With Ace-Queen offsuit in the small blind and the button having folded, I have a strong heads-up hand. Raising is the optimal strategy to take control of the pot, exert pressure on the big blind, and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A raise to 300 is standard and effective for this stack depth.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 2c, which is a very weak and disconnected suited hand. Flashlight raised from the Small Blind. Despite getting 2:1 odds to call, this hand plays very poorly post-flop and won't realize its equity often enough to be profitable. Folding is the best choice.

With Ace-Ten offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game, I have a strong positional advantage and a premium-adjacent hand. Raising is the optimal move here to take control of the pot, put pressure on the blinds, and potentially thin the field. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is standard for this stage of the tournament.

We are in the Small Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. Our hand, 64 suited, is a weak one-gapper. Playing this out of position against a strong pre-flop raise is unprofitable in the long run. We lack the equity and positional advantage to call, and this hand is too weak to use as a 3-bet bluff reliably. The optimal play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h 4h. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind has folded. Although 3h 4h are suited connectors, they are very low in rank, and I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position against a deep-stacked, aggressive opponent. The pot odds are roughly 2.25 to 1, but the equity of 34s against a standard Button opening range is poor, and it is difficult to realize that equity when out of position. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable spot.

With A3o on the button in a 3-handed game, I have a decent ace to try and steal the blinds or play in position post-flop. Raising to 250 is my standard open size from the button, aiming to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot uncontested.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 6c, which is a very weak hand. The player on the Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Defending the small blind with a hand this weak against a button raise is generally unprofitable, especially as I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. While the pot odds are roughly 2.75:1, the playability of 9-6 offsuit is poor. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
Defending the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit against a button raise is standard. The pot odds are favorable to call, and the hand has decent playability post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind and out of position against the pre-flop aggressor on a board with an Ace high hand that has no made pair and minimal backdoor equity. Checking is the standard play here to allow the button to act or to see a free turn card if they check behind.

With Ace high and no direct draws on a Jack-Ten high board, betting into the Big Blind's defending range is risky since this flop connects well with hands they would call pre-flop (such as broadway cards, suited connectors, and medium pocket pairs). Checking behind controls the pot size and allows me to realize what little equity I have, potentially catching an Ace or a 3 on the turn, or picking up a draw.

I am in the big blind with Ace-high, and the board is [Td, Js, 2c, 7c]. My opponent checked the flop, suggesting they may have missed or are playing passively. Given that I have no made hand and minimal equity, betting is not a viable strategy. Checking is the most logical play, as it allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free river card, keeping me in the hand without committing more chips with a weak holding.

The turn card brings a club, introducing a potential flush draw but otherwise not changing the board texture much. Flashlight has checked twice, which typically indicates a weak pair, a draw, or giving up. While a delayed continuation bet could fold out better Ace highs or small pocket pairs, my Ace high still holds some marginal showdown value. Checking keeps the pot small, avoids a potential check-raise if they are trapping or semi-bluffing a draw, and guarantees a free river card.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-high on a board of Td-Js-2c-7c-4h. Both players have checked the flop and the turn, which indicates neither player has a strong hand. Given the showdown value of Ace-high, checking is the optimal strategy. Betting would turn a hand with marginal showdown value into a bluff, which is not profitable against an opponent who has shown passivity. Checking to showdown is the most logical path.

With Ace high on a somewhat dry board and having checked down to the river, my hand possesses marginal showdown value against missed straight and flush draws (like KQ, Q9, 98, or club draws). Betting here would turn my hand into a bluff, which might only fold out worse hands that I already beat or occasionally a very weak pair, but risks getting called by any made pair (like a Ten, Seven, or pocket pair) that Flashlight decided to pot-control. Checking behind is the safest play to realize my equity and try to win the pot at showdown without risking more chips.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with King-four offsuit. While the kicker is weak, King-high hands are generally strong enough to open-raise for a steal attempt from the button in this format. I will place a standard 3x big blind raise to 300 to pressure the blinds and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. If called, I have position for the rest of the hand.

I am in the small blind with JTs (JsTh). Flash has raised to 300 from the button. Calling here is the most standard play to realize my equity and see a flop, as 3-betting out of position against an aggressive button can lead to difficult spots if they 4-bet. The stack depths are healthy, so playing a post-flop game with this hand is reasonable.

I am dealt pocket Queens (QQ) in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, the optimal play is to 3-bet squeeze. Calling would allow a 3-way pot where my hand is vulnerable to overcards (A, K) and makes the hand harder to navigate post-flop. A raise size around 4-5x the original raise (to around 1300-1400) charges the opponents maximally and either takes down the dead money now or builds a large pot with a very strong hand.

I opened from the button with King-four offsuit, and after a call from Flashlight, Pro has put in a large squeeze raise to 1400 from the big blind. King-four offsuit is far too weak to continue against a 3-bet of this size, as it is likely dominated by Pro's range. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Pro's squeeze to 1400 after the button opened and I called is a significant show of strength. Holding Jack-Ten offsuit out of position, I am likely behind Pro's continuing range. Calling would inflate the pot while being out of position, and 4-betting is not a viable strategy with this hand. Folding is the most disciplined and stack-efficient play.

8-4 offsuit is a weak starting hand with very little potential for post-flop value. As I am in the button position and there is no obligation to commit chips, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 43o, which is a very weak, unsuited, and low-ranked hand. The Button folded, leaving just the Big Blind and me. Although I could attempt a steal, 43o has virtually no post-flop playability and very low equity when called or 3-bet. The standard and most profitable play here is to simply fold and save the chips.

K8o is a marginal hand, even on the button. While a steal is possible, it's safer to fold this hand pre-flop to avoid tricky post-flop situations with a weak kicker, especially with both blinds still to act.

Action has folded to me in the Small Blind. I am holding 7-6 offsuit, which is a marginal hand. The Big Blind (Flashlight) is the current chip leader and has shown a tendency to defend their blind and call pre-flop raises in previous hands. Being out of position for the remainder of the hand against a large stack makes this a poor spot to attempt a steal or play a bloated pot with a weak hand. I will choose the low-variance path and fold.

I am on the button with 9h 4d, which is an extremely weak hand. Even in a 3-handed game where opening ranges are wider, 94 offsuit is well below the threshold for a profitable raise. Given the stacks and the activity level of Pro and Flashlight, there is no reason to attempt a steal with such poor equity. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With the button folding, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. My hand, Ten-Seven offsuit, is marginal, but completing the small blind is a standard way to see a flop with a decent price. I want to play cautiously while maintaining my chip lead.

K6o is a marginal hand. Flashlight limped in the small blind. I'm in the big blind and can check to see a free flop in position. There's no need to bloat the pot with a weak offsuit King. Checking is the standard play here.

The board is heavily paired with two Aces, which significantly hits the Big Blind's calling/checking range. Holding only ten-high with no pair or draw, I have essentially zero showdown value. Betting would not be value-oriented, and bluffing into a dry, paired board against a check-back range is high-risk. Checking is the optimal play to maintain pot control and minimize losses.

With bottom pair on an paired Ace-high board (Aces and Sixes with a King kicker), I have decent showdown value but little value to gain by betting. Better hands like an Ace or a pocket pair like 77-TT will call, while most worse hands will simply fold. The board is very dry, so there's no immediate need to protect my equity against draws. Checking back to control the pot size and keep Flashlight's weak range intact is the best approach.

The board is still heavily paired with two Aces after the turn card, and I hold ten-high with no draw or pair. Given that both the opponent and I checked the flop, there is no indication that the opponent is weak, but there is also no reason to turn my hand into a bluff here. Checking is the optimal play to maintain pot control and see the river without further investment in a hand with zero showdown value.

With two pair (Aces and Sixes) and a King kicker, I have a decent hand but limited value to extract by betting. Since Flashlight has checked twice, their range is likely weak, possibly containing smaller pairs, high cards, or weak draws. Betting here might only get action from better hands or cause worse hands to fold. Checking again allows me to control the pot size, see a free river, and potentially induce a bluff on the river from Flashlight's weak range.

The board shows two Aces, a Six, a Four, and a Deuce. I hold Ten-Seven high, which is total air and has zero showdown value. Since both the opponent and I have checked through every street, it is highly likely that neither of us has connected with the board, or the opponent is trapping. Betting here would be pointless as I would only get called by hands that beat me. Checking is the only logical play to reach the showdown without investing more chips into a losing hand.

With two pair (Aces and Sixes) and a King kicker, I have a strong holding given the pre-flop limp and the action checking down to the river. Flashlight has checked three streets, indicating a very weak range, likely high cards, a small pocket pair, or a weak pair like a 4 or a 2. I can check behind to safely get to showdown and win the pot against this weak range, as a value bet is unlikely to get called by many worse hands.

I am holding 2c, 6h, which is an extremely weak hand. With no incentive to play this hand and being the first to act on the button, the most disciplined and strategic decision is to fold rather than wasting chips on a marginal holding.

With 3d 4d in the small blind, the hand is a bit too weak and marginal to play out of position against the Big Blind. While suited connectors can be played occasionally, folding here is a solid standard decision to avoid tricky post-flop spots.

With Ace-Four offsuit on the Button, I have a reasonable hand to attempt a blind steal. A4o plays decently in position and blocks strong Aces, making it a standard Raise First In (RFI) candidate from this position. I'll open to 2.5x the big blind.

I am dealt pocket Tens in the Small Blind, a premium hand in a three-handed game. The Button (Pro) has opened for 250. Calling here would leave me out of position in a multi-way or heads-up pot without the initiative. A 3-bet is the standard play to maximize value and gain control of the hand. Based on previous betting patterns in this tournament, a raise to 900 is a standard and effective sizing to put pressure on the opener and potentially isolate the pot.

My hand (6s, 4d) is extremely weak and has no playability in a 3-bet pot. Facing a raise from the button and a strong 3-bet from the small blind, there is no justification for calling or raising with this holding. Folding is the only sensible option to preserve the stack.

My hand, A4o, is an acceptable open from the button, but it performs poorly against a 3-bet from the Small Blind. Flash's 3-bet indicates strength, and A4o is easily dominated. Calling here would leave me in a tough spot post-flop with a weak kicker, and a 4-bet bluff is unnecessary. Folding is the standard and disciplined play.

I am on the button with Ad6d, a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. My stack is roughly 83 big blinds, which is healthy. Both opponents have similar or larger stacks. My hand, 2c 4d, is extremely weak and has poor playability in a raised pot, especially out of position from the small blind. Calling or raising with such a range-bottom hand against a button raise is negative EV. Folding is the only disciplined and standard play here.
Q6o is a very weak hand to defend from the big blind against a 3x open from the button. Folding is the only sensible choice to preserve my stack.

I am holding 5c 4h on the button. This is a very weak, unsuited hand with poor connectivity and low card values. Even in a 3-handed game, this hand has very low equity and is generally a fold from any position. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten suited, a very strong hand for a blind vs blind situation. Flashlight folded on the button. I want to raise to build the pot, extract value from worse Ax, Tx, or broadway hands, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. I am in the Big Blind with Qh Th, a very strong hand for a blind-versus-blind encounter. Pro raised from the Small Blind to 300. I need to call 200 more to see a flop. Given the pot odds and the playability of my suited connectors in position, calling is the most standard and effective play here.

The flop of 9s 8d Kh hits the Big Blind's calling range quite well, as they can have many combinations of 9x, 8x, straight draws like JT or QJ, and medium pocket pairs. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and two overcards to the middle of the board. Pro, the pre-flop raiser, has checked the King-high flop.
