Since I am in position and have a drawing hand with some showdown value if I hit a pair, checking back to see a free turn card is a safe and effective way to realize my equity without risking a check-raise.

The turn card is the Jack of diamonds, which improves my hand to an open-ended straight draw, needing a Queen or a Seven to complete the straight. My Ace also serves as a strong overcard. While I could bet here as a semi-bluff, checking out of position allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free river card to realize my equity. If my opponent bets, my draw is strong enough to call a reasonably sized bet.

I have completed a straight (8-9-T-J-Q) on the turn, which is currently the nuts. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked on both the flop and the turn. This could indicate he is either waving the white flag with a missed hand or attempting to trap with a strong hand like top pair, two pair, or a set. Since I have the best possible hand and the pot is relatively small at 600 chips, I must bet to build value. A bet of 400 (two-thirds of the pot) is large enough to get value from Kings, Jacks, or straight/flush draws while still being small enough to potentially entice a call from middle pairs.

Facing a 400-chip bet (two-thirds of the pot) from my opponent, I have an open-ended straight draw with my Ace-Ten, giving me 8 clear outs to a straight (any Queen or Seven). Although my direct pot odds (calling 400 to win a total pot of 1400, or about 28.5%) are slightly worse than my chance of hitting the draw on the river (roughly 17.4%), the implied odds make up for this gap. If I hit the nut straight with a Queen or a straight with a Seven, I am highly likely to extract more value on the river. Calling is the most sensible option to realize my equity affordably without overcommitting chips out of position.

I missed my straight draw on the river, only hitting a pair of Tens. The river Ten of Diamonds completes a four-card straight on the board (8-9-T-J) and brings a third diamond. Given the board is now extremely coordinated and dangerous, it is unlikely that a worse hand will call a value bet. Pro could be checking to induce a bluff with a marginal hand or checking a powerhouse (like an Ace or a flush) to check-raise. Checking back allows me to reach showdown and realize the value of my straight without risking a loss to a superior hand.

J5 suited is a very weak hand, even on the button. While it has some flush potential, it lacks high card strength and playability against three-bets or callers. Folding is the most straightforward and solid play here.

I am on the button with 6s3s, a weak suited hand. While suited connectors or gappers can sometimes be played on the button, 63s is at the very bottom of that range. I am in the small blind with suited connectors (7c 6c) and the button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. Completing the small blind is a standard and effective strategy to see a flop cheaply with a speculative hand, avoiding building a large pot out of position pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak, unsuited hand (T5o). The Small Blind just completed the bet. Since I already have my blind posted and no one raised, I can check and see the flop for free in position. There is no need to bloat the pot or attempt a bluff pre-flop with this holding.

I am out of position against the Big Blind with a flush draw (7c 6c) on a board of Qs Ac 5c. I flopped bottom pair on a very wet and high-card heavy board (Qs Ac 5c). Since Flashlight checked to me, checking behind is the best option to control the pot size. I have a pair of sixes with a flush draw on a board of Qs Ac 5c 6h. Betting out might not generate value against worse hands, and I want to see if the opponent is willing to bluff or check back again, allowing me to realize my equity for free.

Flashlight checked again on the turn. I only have bottom pair, which has some marginal showdown value, but betting here won't accomplish much. Better hands will call or raise, and worse hands will fold. Controlling the pot size and seeing a free river is the most prudent play.

I have two pair (sixes and fives) with an ace kicker on a board of Qs Ac 5c 6h 5h. I hit trip Fives on the river after the action checked all the way down. Flashlight's consecutive checks indicate a weak showdown-value hand or a missed draw. I hold a pair of sixes on a board of Qs-Ac-5c-6h-5h. The opponent's bet of 150 into a pot of 350 is small, representing roughly one-third of the pot. Given the favorable pot odds, I have enough equity to justify a call to see a showdown.

I am on the button with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in this position. A standard raise is appropriate here to build the pot, take control of the hand, and potentially win the blinds immediately.

I am in the Small Blind with 4d 6h, which is a very weak, disconnected off-suit hand. The Button (Flashlight) has opened with a raise to 300. Playing such a weak hand out of position is highly unprofitable, so the standard and correct play here is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Eight offsuit. The player on the Button (Flashlight) has opened for 300. With the Small Blind folding, the pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call. On a flop of Qh-Jc-Kc, the board is very heavy with Broadway cards, which significantly favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). The flop of Qh-Jc-Kc is extremely favorable for the pre-flop aggressor's range, as it contains three Broadway cards. Holding pocket eights on the button is a strong starting hand. A standard open-raise to 2.5x or 3x the big blind is the best play here to build the pot, take control of the hand, and potentially win the blinds right away. I'll open to 250.

With Ten-Nine offsuit in the Small Blind, I am facing a 2.5x open from the Button. I am in the big blind holding Ace-King suited, which is a premium hand. Since the button raised, I should 3-bet to build the pot and take control of the hand, rather than just calling. Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind, pocket eights are too strong to fold but usually not strong enough to 4-bet for value. Calling in position allows us to set-mine and evaluate the flop. If we hit a set, we have excellent implied odds against Flashlight's strong 3-betting range.

I hold top pair, top kicker on a board that is somewhat connected but generally favorable for my range as the pre-flop 3-better. Since I have the initiative, I should continue to bet for value against weaker kings or mid-pairs and to protect against potential club draws. Facing a continuation bet on a King-high flop with a flush draw, my pocket eights are in a very weak position against a 3-bettor's range. I am on the button with Js9c, which is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. Raising here allows me to apply pressure to the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. If called, I will have positional advantage for the rest of the hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate given the stack sizes.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. This is a standard defending hand against a Button open raise. My stack is the largest at the table, allowing me to play comfortably. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds and position, and 3-betting with this holding out of position is not optimal. I will call to see a flop and evaluate the situation from there, keeping the pot manageable.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c5c, a low suited one-gapper. Flash raised to 3x the big blind from the Button, and Flashlight called from the Small Blind. I am facing a 200 chip call into a pot of 700. I am in the Small Blind and checked to see this flop against the Button raiser. The board of K-5-2 is very dry and favors the Button's pre-flop raising range significantly. I have nothing but Queen-high and no real draw, so betting into the aggressor is ill-advised. I am the pre-flop aggressor with a 3x raise from the button, and the flop comes K-high (2c Kc 5d). Although I only have Jack-high, I can represent a King or a strong pocket pair effectively. A bet of 350 (half the pot) is enough to force folds from most of Flashlight's range that missed this board, such as small pairs or middle connectors, while keeping my risk controlled if they do have a King and decide to continue.

The board of King-Five-Deuce is extremely dry and heavily favors the Button's pre-flop raising range, as it is very likely they have a King or an overpair. My hand, Queen-Nine, has completely missed the board and provides no equity, not even a backdoor draw or a pair. Calling a continuation bet of 350 into a 700 pot with essentially no equity would be a significant mistake. Folding is the correct professional decision to preserve my stack.

Holding Jack-Nine of hearts on the button, I have a playable suited connector that performs well in a three-handed game. Opening the pot with a raise is the standard and most profitable play from this position to capitalize on the advantage of being the dealer, put pressure on the blinds, and potentially take down the pot immediately or see a flop with the initiative.

Q6o is a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the button, calling from the small blind with this holding would be unprofitable, as we would be playing out of position with a marginal holding. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Six offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind (Pro) has folded. I need to call 200 more into a total pot of 450. This gives me pot odds of approximately 2.25:1, requiring about 31% equity to be profitable. On a paired board of 10s (3s-Th-Tc), the range of hands that hit is relatively narrow. My King-high has some showdown value in a three-handed game but is not strong enough to lead out with on this texture. Checking allows me to evaluate the strength of Flashlight's hand based on their betting sizing and frequency.

The board is paired with Tens, which significantly reduces the likelihood of the opponent holding a strong draw. With Jack-high, I have minimal showdown value but some backdoor potential. Checking back is the most balanced approach here; it keeps the pot small, denies the opponent information, and allows me to see a free turn card while realizing my equity against a range that may have checked a medium-strength hand or is playing cautiously.

The board has paired to Four-of-a-Kind (Tens). In this situation, the best five-card hand for everyone is the four Tens plus the highest kicker available. My Jack-high has no showdown value and betting here would likely only be called by better hands. Checking behind allows me to control the pot, avoid potential check-raises, and see the river for free.

On this board of Four-of-a-Kind (Tens), the best possible hand is determined by the fifth card (the kicker). I hold a King, which means my final hand is T-T-T-T-K. The board shows three Tens, an Eight, and a Trey. My hand is Jack-high. The opponent has checked down through all streets, indicating a lack of a strong hand or a very passive line. Since I only have Jack-high, I have no showdown value against any made hand (like an 8 or a 3) and cannot expect to extract value from a bluff. Checking behind is the most sensible play to minimize risk and reach showdown, as a bet would likely only be called by a hand that beats me.
I am on the Button with 95o, a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. It is not worth stealing the blinds or playing this hand in any way. The standard and most profitable play is to simply fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Eight suited (Js 8s) after the Button has folded. This is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a 3-handed game to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately. My hand is 2c 5c, which is extremely weak. I am in the small blind with King-high on a board of 9d-8h-Qc-Qh. I have no pair and no legitimate draw. Given the low-stakes, heads-up context and my lack of showdown value, checking is the most prudent strategy to keep the pot size manageable and minimize potential losses against the big blind.

I am in the small blind with King-high on a board of 9d-8h-Qc-Qh-8c. I am in the Big Blind with a flush draw on a [4h, Js, 5h] board. Being out of position, checking is the standard play to control the pot size while keeping my options open. I am in the Big Blind with a flush draw on a [4h, Js, 5h, Tc] board. Since the flop was checked around, Pro has likely shown some weakness or passivity. With King-high and a gutshot straight draw, I have some equity but checking again is a safe and reasonable play to see a free river. I am in the Big Blind with 9h, 7h on a board of 4h, Js, 5h, Tc, Jd. The action has checked through on the flop and turn. I have nine-high with no pair and no flush. On the Button with King-Four suited in a 3-handed game, this is a standard opening hand. My typical raise size throughout the tournament has been 3x (300 chips), and I will maintain that consistency here to apply pressure on the blinds and utilize my positional advantage.

I am in the small blind with 5-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the button, there is no strategic justification to call or defend this hand out of position. The best course of action is to fold and conserve my stack for a better spot.

A6 suited is a strong hand to defend from the Big Blind against a Button open. I missed this dry rainbow flop with my A6 suited, having no pair and no draw. As the out-of-position pre-flop caller, the standard and most prudent play is to check to the pre-flop raiser to evaluate their action.

I have bottom pair (fours) with a King kicker and a backdoor club flush draw. The flop is relatively dry (Queen-Eight-Four rainbow), which favors the pre-flop aggressor. On a dry 4s 8c Qd flop, we completely missed with our A6 suited, having only Ace high and a backdoor flush draw. Out of position against the pre-flop raiser's continuation bet, our hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify calling. Folding is the disciplined and correct play.

I am in the button position with Ten of Spades and Six of Clubs (T6o). This is a weak, unsuited hand that does not warrant an open from the button, especially given that I have a solid stack and there is no reason to play garbage hands. Folding is the standard and correct strategic play here.

I have bottom pair (deuces) with a King kicker on a very dry board. On the river, the board is 5h Qc 2s 3c Tc. I have bottom pair (deuces) with a King kicker. Pro raised pre-flop and led the flop for 1/3 pot, then checked both the turn and the river. The board texture of 3c-3d-5d is extremely dry and favorable for my pocket Tens. I hold pocket tens on a board of 3c-3d-5d-Jd-4c. My opponent checked the turn and the river. I am on the Button with 6h 2d, an extremely weak, unsuited, and unconnected hand. It is not playable in any position, especially with two players left to act behind me. Folding is the only correct decision here.
